summary: Gruber argues that the podcast industry's push toward programmatic, dynamically inserted advertising is a terrible idea that will replicate the failures of web advertising. He cites cases of inappropriate ads being served and quotes Marco Arment and Ken Kocienda to support the view that host-read ads are superior. He extends the argument to explain why podcasts survived as an RSS-based medium while web RSS feeds died — podcasts had a working ad model (host reads) while web publishers never figured out how to monetize full-content feeds. He concludes that replacing a model that works for everyone with web-style ad tech is pure grift.
key_points: Dynamic programmatic podcast ads are already failing, serving inappropriate ads to kids' shows and violating opt-out preferences
Host-read baked-in podcast ads have better CPMs, response rates, and audience trust than almost any other advertising form
Podcasts survived as an open RSS-based medium precisely because the host-read sponsorship model provided sustainable monetization that web RSS feeds never achieved
The push toward ad tech in podcasts is breaking something that works in favor of something we already know doesn't work
Gruber's own experience monetizing Daring Fireball's RSS feed with weekly sponsorships — still working 14 years later — proves the host-read model's durability
money_quotes: Even when the "right" ads are dynamically inserted, the ads are inevitably going to be bad. We know how this story ends because we all use the web and can see with our own eyes the quality (and oppressive quantity) of "ad tech" advertising.
There's never been a form of advertising more despised than today's online web advertising.
The ads are part of the product.
I've built the entire business model of Daring Fireball around that sentiment.
The entire podcast universe is built on RSS.
Putting only article excerpts in an RSS feed makes no more sense than putting only audio excerpts into a podcast feed.

Trying to move podcasts to web-like "industry standard advertising" is worse than violating the spirit of If it ain't broke don't fix it — this is breaking something that definitely works for something we know doesn't.
It's grift on the part of the ad industry, pure and simple.
key_insight: Podcast advertising works precisely because it resists the ad-tech model — host-read ads are part of the product, and replacing them with programmatic insertion is importing the web's worst failure into a medium that got monetization right.
